payload.text:
Design a mobile‑first single‑page web app called “OrGo” for on‑demand home services in Jaipur, India. The app should look and behave like a native app with a fixed red top bar (logo, “OrGo” title, hamburger icon) and a fixed red bottom navigation bar with five tabs: Home, Orders, Offers, Tracking, and Account. Each tab shows its own full‑screen view without page reloads; only one main view is visible at a time.Unauthenticated users first see a full‑screen login/signup experience. Center a white card with the OrGo logo, title, short subtitle, and two flows: “Create Account” (signup) and “Login” for existing users. Signup uses fields for full name, email, Indian phone (+91), password, and confirm password, plus optional “Continue with Google/Apple” buttons (these can be dummy). Login accepts phone/email and password, with a “Continue with Google/Apple” option. The “Create Account” and “Login” links instantly switch between the two views. After a successful login/signup, hide the auth view entirely and show the Home tab as the main app; later, Logout in Account returns to this login screen.On the Home tab (for logged‑in users), show:A location bar indicating Jaipur with a location pin, “Jaipur” badge, and dropdown arrow (location change is non‑functional for now).A red gradient hero banner promoting “Get 50% OFF on your first service booking in Jaipur” with a “Use Code: ORGO50” callout.A “What do you need help with?” heading and a responsive grid of service cards (Appliance Repair, Plumbing, Carpentry, Electrical, Vehicle Repair, Gardening, Housekeeping, etc.). Each card shows an icon, service name, and “From ₹price”. Tapping a card starts a booking flow for that service.Booking is a multi‑step wizard shown in a dedicated “Booking” view:Select service (dropdown pre‑filled from the clicked card).Select address in Jaipur: “Use Current Location” button (just fills a demo address) and a text box to type address.Describe problem and choose date + time slot (Morning/Afternoon/Evening/Night).Choose professional from a list of cards with name, rating, jobs done, distance, and ETA; selecting one highlights it.Payment & promo: choose payment method, show a small cost breakdown (service fee, travel fee, GST, discount, total), allow entering a promo code like ORGO50/PLUMB100/FREECALL and visually update discount and total when valid.Review & confirm: show a summary of service, address, date/time, professional, and final price with a “Confirm Booking” button. After confirmation, show a success screen with a large check icon, a generated booking ID, and buttons “Track Order”, “View Details”, and “Back to Home”.The Orders tab lists past and current bookings as cards showing service name, status badge (e.g., Active/Completed), key details (professional, date/time, price, address), and action buttons: “Track” (go to Tracking for that booking), “Rate” (open a simple rating interaction), and “Cancel” (show a confirmation then mark as cancelled visually).The Offers tab shows a top red banner repeating the 50% Jaipur offer and a grid/list of coupon cards. Each card has an offer title, short description, minimum order text, and a promo code with a “Copy” button.The Tracking tab focuses on a single active booking. At the top, reserve area for a map that visually shows “Your Location in Jaipur” and “Professional location” with a moving marker (can be simulated). Below the map, display ETA in minutes, approximate distance, and two buttons: “Call” and “Message” (simple actions). Add a vertical timeline with steps such as Booking Confirmed, Professional Assigned, On the Way, and Service Completed, using different colors for completed/active steps.The Account tab shows a profile card with a circular avatar (first letter of user name), full name, email, and an “Edit Profile” button (can be placeholder). Below it, show a vertical list of menu items: My Bookings, Saved Addresses, Payment Methods, Settings, Help & Support (each can show a simple placeholder action). At the bottom, include a full‑width “Logout” button which returns the user to the login/signup screen and hides all internal pages.

**Define Application Scope**

I'm currently focused on defining the initial scope of "OrGo". The user wants a mobile-first, single-page React application, which is my main objective. I'm prioritizing understanding the core features and design elements. I'm focusing on the request for a red color scheme and bottom navigation with five tabs. I'm also considering the location in Jaipur and its implications.


**Prioritize Core Features**

I've moved on to the tech stack. I'm planning to use React 18+ with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Recharts, including `lucide-react` for icons. I'm focusing on the user's detailed requirement for a Firebase-powered authentication flow and the structure of UI features across different tabs (Home, Orders, Offers, Tracking, Account) as well as a multi-step booking wizard. I am trying to balance the needs for data visualization and the general need for a user-friendly UI.


**Structuring UI Elements**

Now, I'm focusing on the component structure and data models. I'm building reusable UI pieces for the top and bottom bars, authentication forms, and the main layout manager that handles authentication checks and routing. I'm also preparing for the multi-step booking wizard with dedicated components for each step. My current priority is to define the `types.ts` file.


**Devising Component Architecture**

I'm now fully immersed in the component structure. I've broken down the application into individual components, including the core tabs (Home, Orders, Offers, Tracking, and Account) and the crucial booking wizard. I'm focusing on their functionality and how they interact.


**Adjusting App State**

I'm currently focused on persistent Firebase auth state and the trigger for the booking wizard. I'm addressing the issue of persisting the Firebase authentication state with `onAuthStateChanged` in `App.tsx`. I'm also now managing a `currentView` state, which will enable the smooth navigation to the booking wizard when a card is clicked in the Home tab, switching the view to 'booking'. Also, I will use `recharts` for a "Monthly Spending" chart in the Account tab, since this satisfies the library requirement nicely.


**Refining Component Interactions**

I'm now fully immersed in the component details. I'm focusing on the structural details that tie the user interface together. The `App.tsx` file now defines several new states (`user`, `loading`, `activeTab`, `showBooking`, `selectedService`) that dictate the flow. `Auth.tsx` is being refined to handle login, signup, and forgot password scenarios and also error conditions. The modularity of `Home.tsx` and the timeline visualization in `Tracking.tsx` are coming together, and the flow of the booking wizard is in focus.


**Reviewing Final Code**

I'm now deeply immersed in the final phases. I'm rigorously verifying the code against the requested XML structure. I'm focusing on ensuring all imports are correct and that I've avoided any potential infinite loops within the dependency arrays. I'm also now focusing on the Firebase imports, making sure I use the v9+ modular syntax. Finally, I'm verifying the prominence of "Jaipur" and adding mock data, as well as checking the structure for modularity. I'm happy with how the app's building.

To "localhost" this app using Firebase (specifically the **Firebase Local Emulator Suite**), you need to build your React app into static files and then tell Firebase to serve those files.

Here is the step-by-step guide and the necessary configuration files.

### 1. Update `index.html` for Building
The `index.html` you provided currently uses an `importmap` for CDNs. Since you are setting up a local build environment with `package.json` and Vite, we need to remove the CDNs so the build tool bundles your local dependencies correctly.

### 2. Add Firebase Configuration Files
We need to add `firebase.json` (hosting configuration) and `.firebaserc` (project mapping).

payload.text:
### Step-by-Step Instructions to Run Locally

Open your terminal in the project folder and run these commands in order:

**1. Install Dependencies**
Install the libraries defined in your `package.json`.
```bash
npm install
```

**2. Install Firebase CLI (Global)**
If you haven't installed the Firebase tools yet:
```bash
npm install -g firebase-tools
```

**3. Login to Firebase**
This connects your terminal to your Google account.
```bash
firebase login
```

**4. Build the Application**
This uses Vite to compile your TypeScript and React code into a `dist` folder (the static website).
```bash
npm run build
```

**5. Start the Firebase Emulator**
This mimics the actual Firebase Hosting environment on your computer.
```bash
firebase emulators:start --only hosting
```

**6. View the App**
The terminal will show you a "Local server" URL, usually:
`http://localhost:5000`

Open that link in your browser to see your app running exactly as it would on the web.

payload.text:
### 2. Run These Commands
Open your terminal in the project folder and run these three commands in order. This will build your app and push it to the internet.

**Step 1: Build the App**
This converts your code into the `dist` folder, ready for the web.
```bash
npm run build
```

**Step 2: Login to Firebase**
If you aren't logged in, this will open a browser window to authorize your Google account.
```bash
firebase login
```

**Step 3: Deploy to Internet**
This uploads the `dist` folder to Firebase Hosting.
```bash
firebase deploy
```

Once finished, the terminal will give you a **Hosting URL** (e.g., `https://orgo-e6ff8.web.app`). You can share this link with anyone to use the app.

Everytime a user registers, add them to my firestore database collection '/users'. Path: "/users/{{uid}}".

If a user is already registered but isn't in the database, add them there when they log in. Add all necessary fields into their document: name, email, photo file name.

When a user signs in, display all their profile information from their document in the profile ui. Let users edit their profile information, delete their account and update records accordingly

**Refining the Firebase Plan**

I'm now zeroing in on integrating Firebase Firestore to manage user details in OrGo. My focus is on creating a `/users/` collection. Each user doc will be created during signup, but the app should also handle updates gracefully. Key user fields like display name, email, and maybe their organization identifier are the priorities here. I'm exploring the implications for read/write rules to secure the data.


**Solidifying Firestore Integration**

I'm solidifying how to integrate Firebase Firestore. I'll utilize `/users/{{uid}}` for each user. Upon login or signup, the system will verify and create Firestore entries if missing. Key fields like `name`, `email`, and possibly `photo` are planned. I'm focusing on profile UI updates to display and modify data from Firestore, with a focus on account deletion and appropriate record updates.


**Planning Component Refactor**

I'm now determining how to handle the Account view, weighing the options of refactoring it as its own dedicated component, or expanding its logic in the existing `App.tsx` file. Considering the complexity of editing, viewing, and deleting account details, I've decided to create a separate `components/Account.tsx` file to handle the logic, while `App.tsx` maintains the routing logic. This decision prioritizes a cleaner and more maintainable code structure for future expansions.


**Developing User Data Management**

I'm presently building out the user data management in Firestore based on my prior design. For sign-ups, I'll `setDoc` with the user's details, including name and email, and I'm adding `createdAt` too. When a user logs in, I'll check for an existing document; if it's missing, I create one with `setDoc`. For profile editing, I'm integrating inputs for name and phone with Firestore updates. Alongside the Firestore update, I'm now adding Firebase Auth profile updates to keep data synchronized. Account deletion will start with deleting the Auth account and then delete the corresponding Firestore record. For the photo file name, I plan to store a URL.


**Integrating Photo URLs**

I've decided to handle the user photo URL as a simple text field in Firestore. There are ongoing code changes, and I've focused on `firebase.ts` by adding `getFirestore`. Also, I'm working to integrate the Firestore creation logic within `Auth.tsx`. I will replace the inline render in `App.tsx` with a `<Account>` component. I also need to update `UserProfile` within `types.ts` to include sync fields, and I'm updating profile after `createUserWithEmailAndPassword` to keep the data consistent.


**Building Account Functionality**

I'm now implementing the `Account` component. I will use `useEffect` to fetch user data from Firestore on component mount, and manage state for `userData` and editing mode. The UI will render inputs or text based on the editing state, and "Save" and "Delete Account" buttons will trigger the respective Firestore and Auth actions. I will need to refactor `firebase.ts` to export `db`. I'll also modify `handleAuth` in `Auth.tsx` to include Firestore interaction and replace `renderAccount` in `App.tsx` with the new component. Lastly, I'll update `types.ts` to accommodate the user data fields.


**Organizing Data Sync**

I'm now prioritizing keeping the user data consistent across Firebase Auth and Firestore. Specifically, I'm integrating a check in `Auth.tsx`'s `LOGIN` flow to ensure a Firestore document exists for every authenticated user. This will include creating a document if needed. I'm starting to formalize the Firestore integration within `firebase.ts`, preparing for data fetching and updates within the `Account` component. I will update the XML accordingly for the files listed. The `Account` component design is solidifying, focusing on fetching user data, providing edit/save functionality, and displaying name, email, photo (via URL), and phone number.


**Correcting Auth Flow**

I realize that I should await the Firestore write operations in `Auth.tsx` to ensure data consistency before calling `onLoginSuccess`. I also understand the need for the `Account` component to fetch user data independently from `App.tsx`. I'm going to add `getFirestore` in `firebase.ts` and export the `db` variable for other modules. I plan to handle the photo as a string URL field in the database.


**Updating Login Logic**

I'm now refining the login flow in `Auth.tsx` to handle user data. After authentication, I check for a corresponding Firestore document. If none exists, I create one with the user's name, email, and photo URL. Await calls ensure data consistency before onLoginSuccess runs.


**Structuring Firestore Data**

I'm now focusing on the structure of data in Firestore, ensuring that user data, including the phone number and `createdAt` field are properly incorporated into document creation within the authentication flow. I am also working on rendering the appropriate inputs in the `Account` component.
Here is the implementation to integrate Firestore for user management, including syncing on registration/login and a full profile management UI.

**Investigating Import Errors**

I'm currently looking into the `firebase/firestore` import errors flagged in `firebase.ts`, `components/Auth.tsx`, and `components/Account.tsx`. The specific functions `getFirestore`, `doc`, `setDoc`, `getDoc`, `updateDoc`, and `deleteDoc` seem to be the source of the issues. I'm focusing on why these aren't being recognized during compilation, likely a version mismatch or incorrect import path.


**Debugging Import Issues**

I've been concentrating on the import errors in `firebase.ts`, `components/Auth.tsx`, and `components/Account.tsx`, particularly with `firebase/firestore`. The code strongly suggests v9 modular syntax.  I'm exploring potential issues: a mismatched Firebase SDK version, incorrect import paths, or a TypeScript/Firebase version incompatibility. The error "Module 'firebase/firestore' has no exported member '...'" is leading me to check type resolutions and package configurations.  My current focus is verifying correct Firebase v9 imports, like `import { getFirestore } from "firebase/firestore";` as opposed to any specific online editor configuration.


**Clarifying Firebase Version**

I'm now investigating a discrepancy between the Firebase version implied by the code and the possible installed version. The presence of `initializeApp` from `firebase/app` strongly suggests Firebase v9, yet the import errors with `firebase/firestore` hint at an older version. It seems the user might be trying to use v9 modular syntax with an older Firebase install. I'm considering scenarios where the environment might be providing the wrong version or where the user's dependencies are conflicting.


**Diagnosing Version Conflict**

I'm checking if the environment is misconfigured or if there's a conflict in dependencies or mocks.  I'm also considering potential issues related to the user's environment expecting a different syntax.

Another consideration is an alternate import path like `firebase/firestore/lite`, or verifying that the correct import, `import { getFirestore } from "firebase/firestore";` has been used. I'm examining the possibility that the project is unintentionally using a reduced-functionality version of Firestore. payload.text: Everytime a user order, choose service then location, then cost and time, add them to my firestore database collection '/order'. Path: "/order/{{uid}}".

**Defining the Data Flow**

I'm currently focused on defining the data flow for saving booking orders.  The `BookingWizard` component in my app is the central point for this. I'm aiming to trigger the Firestore write operation at the end of the wizard's process. The challenge is ensuring the data is correctly structured before the save operation.


**Investigating Data Persistence**

The recent focus has been on the `BookingWizard`'s role in the Firestore save operation. I'm exploring the implications of the `/order/{{uid}}` path, particularly whether it implies a user-specific subcollection or a root 'orders' collection. Several interpretations exist, and I'm now leaning towards the more standard `orders/{orderId}` pattern, or possibly `users/{userId}/orders/{orderId}`, to ensure a robust and scalable data structure. The goal is to solidify this data storage approach.


**Analyzing Data Structure Implications**

I'm now grappling with the implications of the provided path specification. The `Path: /order/{{uid}}` instruction strongly suggests storing orders with the user's ID as the document ID, but the phrase "Everytime a user order" implies multiple orders per user. This contradicts direct interpretation. I suspect the user may be conflating collection and document paths. Currently, I'm examining how to generate unique order IDs within a single `order` collection and associate each order with the corresponding user ID within the document's data.


**Interpreting User Intent**

I've been re-evaluating the best data structure. While the prompt's `Path: /order/{{uid}}` initially suggested a user-specific document, the "Everytime a user order" phrase points to multiple orders per user. I'm now leaning toward storing orders in the `orders` collection, using `addDoc` to auto-generate unique order IDs, but including the `userId` field for user association. The path may have indicated that the user wants to group, not overwrite. I'm choosing to interpret the instructions as a collection for orders and an automatically generated document id.


**Refining Order Persistence**

I'm now revisiting the storage strategy. While the initial path suggested overwriting, the intention seems to be adding multiple orders.  `addDoc` to the `order` collection appears correct, avoiding overwrites. Though the specified path is `/order/{{uid}}`, it feels semantically better to create individual documents within this collection, each with a unique ID, while including the user's ID as a field within the document itself for association. I've ruled out the overwrite and subcollection approaches, considering this structure addresses potential scaling issues and aligns more closely with the inferred requirements for handling multiple user orders.


**Interpreting Path Specificity**

I'm now reconsidering the implications of the `/order/{{uid}}` path's specificity. It makes me question if the intent is to store the currently active order directly under the user's ID, which fits the tracking view idea. However, the app's order statuses and the need for order history complicate this approach.  My current plan is to use `addDoc` in the 'orders' collection to maintain order history. I'm also considering storing the order structure under `users/{uid}/orders/{orderId}`, to stay consistent with the provided path.  


**Resolving Path Ambiguity**

I've re-examined the suggested path and the "collection '/order'" phrasing. Initially, I leaned toward a root `orders` collection with `userId` data in each document.  However, to respect the directive, I'm now adopting a structure where orders are stored within a `users/{userId}/orders` subcollection using `addDoc` to generate unique order IDs. This balances path compliance and history preservation. I believe this structure best aligns with the combined instructions.


**Prioritizing Structure Consistency**

I've been re-evaluating the best path forward. I'm now leaning away from the `users/{userId}/orders/{orderId}` subcollection approach as it adds unnecessary complexity. I'm choosing to stick with a root collection of `orders` and `addDoc`. The focus now is on confirming the user's intent. While the initial instruction implies otherwise, a root level `orders` with unique document IDs seems like a sensible approach. This design is robust enough to accommodate order history, unique order generation, and direct association with user IDs.


**Saving Order Details**

I'm now focusing on implementing the order save logic within `BookingWizard.tsx`. I plan to utilize `addDoc` to the `orders` collection, including the `userId` in each document. I'm prioritizing functionality over strict path adherence; the provided path structure might suggest subcollections, but that seems unnecessarily complex. My priority is to save the order data comprehensively, including service details, cost, time, address, professional, status, timestamp, and user ID. I've considered that the prompt may have a complex nesting that goes from `/order/{{uid}}`, but ultimately decided that saving to `collection(db, 'orders')` using `addDoc` is the way forward, for now.


**Clarifying Order Storage**

I'm now revisiting the storage strategy. While the path points to `/order/{{uid}}`, the goal is to store multiple orders. Currently, I'm using `addDoc` to the `orders` collection, but storing the `userId` within each document. This addresses history and user association. Now, I'm considering fetching the order data in `App.tsx` and displaying it. The focus has been on persisting data in `BookingWizard.tsx`, but now I think about retrieving it.


**Optimizing Data Persistence**

The recent focus has been on persisting the order data within the 'orders' collection. I've chosen to use `addDoc`, ensuring auto-generated document IDs and incorporating the `userId` in each document. This addresses history and user association needs. Additionally, I'm now exploring how to retrieve the persisted order data in `App.tsx` for display.


**Enhancing Data Retrieval**

I'm now integrating order data retrieval in `App.tsx`. My current focus is shifting to reading from the Firestore `orders` collection based on the current user's ID. I plan to replace the `MOCK_ORDERS` data with the real-time order data and display it appropriately. The primary emphasis is to facilitate data persistence and data visibility by using a read operation.


**Finalizing Order Storage**

I'm now implementing the order persistence logic within `BookingWizard.tsx`.  I'll be using `addDoc` to the `orders` collection with the user's ID included within each document. This addresses history and user association needs. Additionally, I'm now exploring how to retrieve the persisted order data in `App.tsx` for display. I plan to use `query` with `where` clauses.


**Adapting to SDK Limitations**

I'm now refining the data fetch logic in `App.tsx` due to `firestore/lite`'s constraints. I had planned for real-time updates with `onSnapshot`, but this isn't available in `lite`. My current plan is to use `getDocs` to fetch orders once upon app load or upon switching to the "Orders" tab. I'm choosing a straightforward approach to respect the limitations of the SDK and to deliver the requested features.


**Prioritizing Feature Verification**

I'm now prioritizing the read operation in `App.tsx` so users can see their bookings. It's essential to verify order persistence. The prompt indicates "add them to...", which implies a write. Previously, I established the read, so now I'm prioritizing the write to enable user verification of the new order. The focus is now on showing newly added order entries in the UI.



**Confirming Data Integrity**

I'm revisiting the storage strategy again. Despite the initial `/order/{{uid}}` path, the requirement to support multiple orders suggests a collection-based approach. While a subcollection structure within `users/{uid}/orders` might seem plausible, the added complexity of managing subcollections with `lite` outweighs the benefit. My current plan involves a root `orders` collection, generating unique order IDs with `addDoc`, and storing the user ID as a field within each order document for association.


**Integrating Real-time Updates**

I'm now focusing on integrating real-time order updates in `App.tsx` and verifying the data persistence by displaying the stored order data.  I'll leverage the `useEffect` hook to fetch data. The goal is to provide a seamless user experience, but it also raises new questions about how frequently the data should be fetched and how to handle potential data inconsistency. Now, I'm integrating the read.


**Integrating Firestore Updates**

I'm now focusing on integrating the Firestore updates into my application. Specifically, I'm working on refactoring `App.tsx` to fetch the order data from Firestore. I've removed the mock data and integrated the fetch logic using `getDocs` within a `useEffect` hook. The current emphasis is now on displaying the persisted order data in the `App` component after ensuring a successful write operation within the `BookingWizard` component.


**Handling Data Retrieval**

I'm now switching to retrieving the persisted order data within `App.tsx`. The aim is to make the retrieved data from the database visible and usable within the component. After the addition of new data, the goal is to make the data visible. This focuses on displaying data within the application using a read operation.


**Resolving Collection Specificity**

I've been re-evaluating the collection strategy and path.  I've decided to move forward with `collection(db, 'orders')`, fulfilling the direct instruction. To best interpret "collection '/order'", I'll store the orders in a root `orders` collection. I plan to handle the multiple orders per user requirement by using `addDoc` to create unique document IDs and associating orders with users through the `userId` field within each document.  I believe this aligns with the spirit of the instruction.


**Persisting Data Effectively**

I'm implementing the write logic for the Firestore operations. Utilizing `addDoc` to store order details, as well as handling data retrieval with a direct focus on showing added and updated values, is now the focus. The storage strategy adheres to the most recent directions: use `collection(db, 'order')` to best interpret the instructions.

To satisfy your request, I will update the `BookingWizard` to save new bookings to a Firestore collection named `order`. I will also update the `App` component to fetch and display these real orders instead of the mock data.

I will interpret "Path: /order/{{uid}}" as a directive to associate the order with the user's ID, which I will do by including the `userId` field in the document (the standard scalable approach for NoSQL databases to allow multiple orders per user).

Here are the changes:

1.  **BookingWizard.tsx**: Implement the Firestore `addDoc` logic when the user confirms the booking.
2.  **App.tsx**: Implement `getDocs` to fetch the user's order history from the `order` collection.

entries.description: Save booking data to Firestore 'order' collection on confirmation
Fetch real orders from Firestore 'order' collection
